figures.caption: FIG. 3. (a) Schematics of the circuit realizing the Hamiltonian by Eq. (11) and describing a d = 4 qudit (ququart). It is composed by a capacitance C, an inductance L, a Josephson junction with energy ELπ 2/(4 √ 2) and four, nominally equal, modular elements–each composed by a Josephson junction and an inductance connected in series. All the branches are connected in parallel, with a flux π/2 between the four equal modular elements. The latter effectively yield a π/2-periodic Josephson junction with energy EJ . (b) Spectrum (left panel) and potential and few eigenfunctions (right panel) of the Hamiltonian Eq. (11) as a function of φx. (c) Schematics of the circuit realizing the Hamiltonian by Eq. (12) and describing a d = 5 qudit (ququint). The system features a Josephson junction with energy ELπ 2/8, a π-periodic Josephson junction (symbolized by a crossed-hatched square) with energy ELπ 2/32, and a π/2-periodic Josephson junction with energy EJ , all connected in parallel. The π/2-periodic element can be either obtain as in (a) or via two π-periodic elements connected in parallel and with a flux π/2 between the two. (d) Spectrum (left panel) and potential and few eigenfunctions (right panel) of the Hamiltonian Eq. (12) as a function of φx. In (b) and (d) the parameters chosen are EC/EJ = 0.01 and EL/EJ = 0.04.
FIG. 9. Comparison of the potential realizing a qutrit, its quadratic expansion around φ = 0, and the effective cosine potential ĒJ [1− cos(Lφ)].
